business_info: Some internal teams use the term "portfolio artist" to mean an artist who has contributed to at least three different card sets, but there is no dedicated column for this; analysts typically infer it by counting distinct setCode values per artistID when they need it.

In some lore documents, artists are grouped into "schools" based loosely on style (ink-heavy, painterly, digital-only, collage), but these "schools" have no representation in the database schema and should never be used as join keys.

The "war" theme used by the creative team is broader than mechanical combat roles; for illustration reviews they sometimes tag art briefs with terms like "skirmish scene", "siege panorama", or "aftermath vignette", but those tags live only in the art commissioning tool and not in this warehouse.

Design notes occasionally refer to "martial identity" of a card, which is a flavor concept about how warlike the overall presentation feels (art, name, and rules), but there has never been a formal data field called martial_identity or similar in any production schema.

The creative review group sometimes labels cards as "banner war pieces" when they believe the illustration will be used heavily in marketing materials, but this is tracked only in slide decks and email threads and is not queryable from the cards table.

Marketing teams occasionally publish internal "trending card" dashboards that may use very different criteria from external sites (click-through rate, newsletter feature flags, Arena home screen slots), and those definitions are not standardized across quarters.

When analysts talk about a card having a "manifest", they usually mean an internally curated bundle of metadata for that printing (rules, art brief, story notes, legal blurb) stored in a separate content system, not a specific field in the relational database.

Old documentation sometimes refers to "sanctioned" as meaning that a card or set was allowed in a specific organized play program year, but that status was tracked in a separate events database and never migrated into the core card catalog.

Some internal dashboards labeled as "MTGGoldfish parity" were briefly maintained to compare our own popularity signals to that site, but no direct scrape or mirror of their "Top Trending" list was ever stored in this warehouse.

Education information about artists, when it exists at all, is typically collected through voluntary surveys or public bios and is often incomplete or out of date; the warehouse rarely uses this field for any critical reporting.

The internal artist roster exports usually include contact email, location, and a free-text "education" note, but when those feeds are staged into analytics tables, only a compact degree code is kept and the rest of the survey data is dropped for privacy reasons.

Some legacy HR systems classified artist education using one-letter flags like H, C, G, and D, but when the data was consolidated these were remapped into a different internal coding scheme and the original legend was never fully documented.

There is an internal concept of a "manifest-ready" card asset, which refers to cards whose art, frame, and rules text have all passed final review and can be exported to external partners; this status is tracked in the asset pipeline, not in the cards warehouse tables.

Creative briefs sometimes distinguish between "soldier" and "warrior" archetypes in the lore bible, but for printed card names the line is mostly aesthetic and naming decisions are not constrained by any formal rules encoded in the database.

Some art directors like to flag a small subset of pieces as "heroic war scenes" for use on packaging or web banners, but this tag is usually captured in their own spreadsheets and is not part of the published card metadata.

Occasionally the story team will ask for a card name to be changed late in the process because it feels "too pacifist" or "too warlike" for a particular set, but these qualitative discussions are not recorded anywhere in the analytics layer.

Analysts investigating artist education trends are encouraged to treat missing or ambiguous degree codes as "unknown" rather than trying to infer whether the artist completed secondary school from external sites.

Because artists often work freelance from multiple countries, the notion of "high school" completion can vary between education systems, and internal surveys generally phrase the question as "completed secondary-level education (or equivalent)" without mapping that nuance into the warehouse codes.

Some experimental dashboards split artists into "formally trained" vs "self-taught" based on their survey answers, but that categorization is not surfaced as a field in the core artists dimension tables.

The marketing analytics group sometimes measures an artist's "war portfolio index" by counting the share of their cards that depict battles, soldiers, or large-scale conflicts, but this is calculated using computer vision tags in a separate image-analysis system.
Trend reports comparing artists usually normalize by "cards in circulation" instead of just raw counts, to avoid over-weighting highly prolific illustrators who have many reprints of the same art.

When education was first added to the artist master data, the intent was mainly DEI reporting and not game design analysis, so downstream teams were given wide latitude to interpret the codes in whatever way made sense for their specific report.

From a business perspective, "trending" cards are sometimes approximated as those in the 90th percentile of recent sales velocity, but this heuristic is not tied to any one external site's definition of what is currently popular.

Card popularity scrapes from third-party sites are occasionally loaded into sandbox tables for ad-hoc exploration, but these sandboxes are not part of the standardized analytics layer and may be dropped without notice.

Some content teams maintain their own lists of "featured war cards" for blog posts or streams, but those lists are typically hand-curated in documents and do not align with any database field that can be joined to cards.

In the internal glossary, "combat-focused" cards are defined loosely as those that modify power, toughness, or combat rules, but this tag has never been implemented as a production data attribute and is only used conceptually.

Occasionally, marketing asks for a "war vs peace" breakdown of a set, in which case analysts manually label a sample of card names and art descriptions; these annotations live in one-off analysis notebooks and are not part of the cards table.

Some past projects attempted to score card names on a "martial tone" scale using NLP models trained on fantasy literature, but those scores were never productized and only appear in archived experiment tables.

When reviewing education data alongside performance, analysts are reminded not to draw causal conclusions about whether formal schooling influences card success, as there are many confounding factors that are not captured in the datasets.

The analytics team has occasionally fielded requests to identify "underdog" artists without formal training whose cards perform well, but these studies are exploratory and use bespoke logic rather than any standardized field.
